```julia
julia> y = [ones(2,3) for _=1:4];  # CORRECT BEHAVIOR

julia> stack(y, dims=1) |> size
(4, 2, 3)

julia> stack(y, dims=2) |> size
(2, 4, 3)

julia> stack(y, dims=3) |> size
(2, 3, 4)

julia> y = AbstractArray[ones(2,3) for _=1:4]; # NOW THE PROBLEM

julia> stack(y, dims=1) |> size
(4, 2, 3)

julia> stack(y, dims=2) |> size # THIS IS WRONG
(2, 3, 4)

julia> stack(y, dims=3) |> size
(2, 3, 4)

julia> stack(y, dims=2) == stack(y, dims=2) # THIS IS WRONG
true
```

s]"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-12-07T08:19:21Z","updated_at":"2024-12-07T22:29:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The following issue is present on both julia 1.10.7 and 1.11.2

```julia
julia> x = [ones(2,3) for _=1:4];  # CORRECT BEHAVIOR

julia> stack(x, dims=1) |> size
(4, 2, 3)

julia> stack(x, dims=2) |> size
(2, 4, 3)

julia> stack(x, dims=3) |> size
(2, 3, 4)

julia> y = AbstractArray[ones(2,3) for _=1:4]; # NOW THE PROBLEM

julia> stack(y, dims=1) |> size
(4, 2, 3)

julia> stack(y, dims=2) |> size # THIS IS WRONG
(2, 3, 4)

julia> stack(y, dims=3) |> size
(2, 3, 4)

julia> stack(y, dims=2) == stack(y, dims=3) # THIS IS WRONG
true
```

With `y = Any[ones(2,3) for _=1:4]` no problems instead.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56771/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56771/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2525329617","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56771#issuecomment-2525329617","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56771","id":2525329617,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6WhXjR","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-07T22:29:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-07T22:29:25Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I read the docs as saying that `HasLength` doesn't mean there isn't a shape, only that you don't know it from the type. documentation"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-05T17:13:20Z","updated_at":"2024-12-13T22:36:57Z","closed_at":"2024-12-06T06:28:19Z","author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/pulls/1141","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/pull/1141","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/pull/1141.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/pull/1141.patch","merged_at":"2024-12-06T06:28:19Z"},"body":"Currently, the `DimensionMismatch` exceptions refer to internal parameter names of `gemv!`, which is confusing because those don't necessarily match the caller's variables.  For example:
```jl
julia> B = rand(1000,100); c = rand(1000);

julia> B * c
ERROR: DimensionMismatch: second dimension of A, 100, does not match length of x, 1000
Stacktrace:
 [1] gemv!
   @ ~/.julia/juliaup/julia-1.11.2+0.x64.apple.darwin14/share/julia/stdlib/v1.11/LinearAlgebra/src/matmul.jl:438 [inlined]
```
This PR changes the error messages to refer to "matrix" instead of "A" and "input vector" instead of "x", etc.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1141/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1141/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/comments/2542498166","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/pull/1141#issuecomment-2542498166","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1141","id":2542498166,"node_id":"IC_kwDONPNfxc6Xi3F2","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-13T22:36:56Z","updated_at":"2024-12-13T22:36:56Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","body":"Seems like a good idea. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56822","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56822/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56822/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56822/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56822","id":2739549733,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6jSjYl","number":56822,"title":"Printing of zero-step `StepRangeLen` does not preserve types","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-14T04:14:05Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T04:14:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Here `show` doesn't print something which matches the original:
```julia
julia> x = -1 .+ 0 * range(1,100f0,5)
StepRangeLen(-1.0f0, 0.0f0, 5)

julia> y = StepRangeLen(-1.0f0, 0.0f0, 5)  # copying what's printed
StepRangeLen(-1.0f0, 0.0f0, 5)

julia> x === y  # not ideal!
false

julia> x === StepRangeLen{Float32}(-1.0, 0.0, 5)  # this would be better
true

julia> x === range(-1.0f0, -1.0f0, 5)  # also OK?
true
```
Float64:
```julia
x64 = -1 .+ 0 * range(1,100,5)
y64 = y = StepRangeLen(-1.0, 0.0, 5)  # copying what's printed
x64 === y64  # false
x64 === range(-1.0, -1.0, 5)  # true
```
I'm not certain that printing `range` cannot go wrong.

Originally marked ["ugly temporary printing, to avoid 0:0:0"](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40320/files#diff-8e63081442c6b1785e18b93e97805e8191e62860d828af6d50b530c90b72a847R934-R936).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56822/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56822/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-02-05T14:52:30Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T04:25:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently we need to call the the constructor `PermutedDimsArray` to get non-allocating `permutedims` behaviour. This means we are not free to adjust the interface - we really need to return an actual `PermutedDimsArray`. (This is different to e.g. `view` or `reshape`)

This causes some problems. For example, in DiskArrays.jl we have a custom [`PermutedDiskArray`](https://github.com/meggart/DiskArrays.jl/blob/main/src/permute.jl) generated by `permutedims`, and its lazy (It wont try to print form the cloud into the repl, broadcast over it is lazy, reduce is chunked). But we cant return this from `PermutedDimsArray` without doing _bad things_ in the `PermutedDimsArray` constructor.

DimensionalData.jl is the most common package wrapping a DiskArrays.jl objects (in much of the spatial data sciences) and there we use `PermutedDimsArray` as a low-cost optimisation for permuting in-memory arrays. But this of course doesn't work on DiskArrays.jl as the disk array is no longer visible through the `PermutedDimsArray` wrapper without special-casing.

So I'm proposing 2 possible approaches:
1. A new method like `lazypermutedims` or similar that by default calls `PermutedDimsArray` but we are free to extend however we need to
2. Add a keyword to `permuteddims` of `lazy=false`, and `true` calls `PermutedDimsArray`. This might have some type instability issues.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1896333511","pull_request_review_id":2521402416,"id":1896333511,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5xB8DH","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+function eltype(::Type{Generator{A, typeof(identity)}}) where {A}
+    eltype(A)
+end","path":"base/generator_eltype.jl","commit_id":"5707b5e81f7a916ece1cf72b8b3cea22f7900883","original_commit_id":"5707b5e81f7a916ece1cf72b8b3cea22f7900883","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"TIL that `x for x in A` is noticed by the parser, and produces this type:
```julia
julia> (x for x in 1:3)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, typeof(identity)}(identity, 1:3)

julia> (identity(x) for x in 1:3)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#13#14"}(var"#13#14"(), 1:3)
```
(Was true on Julia 1.6, not true on 1.0.)

I wonder what would break if this instead returned A, i.e. we made this `(x for x in 1:3) === 1:3`. AbstractRange"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-09-27T10:56:32Z","updated_at":"2024-12-24T03:34:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/51475","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51475","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51475.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51475.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR specializes `zero` for known range types to avoid allocation.
```julia
julia> zero(1:5)
StepRangeLen(0, 0, 5)

julia> zero(1:2:5)
StepRangeLen(0, 0, 3)

julia> zero(1.0:2.0:5.0)
StepRangeLen(0.0, 0.0, 3)

julia> zero(LinRange(1, 2, 3))
3-element LinRange{Float64, Int64}:
 0.0, 0.0, 0.0
```
To get this working, I've also added `zero` for `TwicePrecision`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51475/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51475/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2560595714","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51475#issuecomment-2560595714","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51475","id":2560595714,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Yn5cC","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-24T03:34:49Z","updated_at":"2024-12-24T03:34:49Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"For what it's worth, the return types here are the same as `0 * range`:

order"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-11-03T00:53:20Z","updated_at":"2024-11-08T21:34:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56425","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Uses merge sort, as an obvious choice for a stable sort of tuples.

A recursive data structure of singleton type, representing Peano natural numbers, is used to help with splitting a tuple into two halves in the merge sort. An alternative design would use a reference tuple, but this would require relying on `tail` more, which seems more harsh on the compiler. With the recursive datastructure the predecessor operation and the successor operation are both trivial.

Allows inference to preserve inferred element type even when tuple length is not known.

Follow-up PRs may add further improvements, such as the ability to select an unstable sorting algorithm.

The added file, typedomainnumbers.jl is not specific to sorting, thus making it a separate file. Xref #55571.

Fixes #54489","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2465785434","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425#issuecomment-2465785434","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425","id":2465785434,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6S-OZa","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-08T21:34:16Z","updated_at":"2024-11-08T21:34:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I agree this seems like a lot of code, compared to [these 20 lines](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54494/files#diff-7af133c263575ae298b5b0747b2ee9593e87eef99222ed17bfe458520a75e709R1773-R1793) which handle NTuples in #54494 . What exactly is gained, in what use cases? It seems you claim better inference, can you show examples where this matters?

> How about copy pasting the SortedNetwork implementations up to length 16 and then allocate above that?

I assume this refers to https://github.com/JeffreySarnoff/SortingNetworks.jl , which [writes out the steps](https://github.com/JeffreySarnoff/SortingNetworks.jl/blob/main/src/swapsort.jl#L67-L78) for each length. order"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-11-03T00:53:20Z","updated_at":"2024-11-08T23:49:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56425","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Uses merge sort, as an obvious choice for a stable sort of tuples.

A recursive data structure of singleton type, representing Peano natural numbers, is used to help with splitting a tuple into two halves in the merge sort. An alternative design would use a reference tuple, but this would require relying on `tail` more, which seems more harsh on the compiler. With the recursive datastructure the predecessor operation and the successor operation are both trivial.

Allows inference to preserve inferred element type even when tuple length is not known.

Follow-up PRs may add further improvements, such as the ability to select an unstable sorting algorithm.

The added file, typedomainnumbers.jl is not specific to sorting, thus making it a separate file. Xref #55571.

Fixes #54489","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2465911111","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425#issuecomment-2465911111","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425","id":2465911111,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6S-tFH","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-08T23:49:53Z","updated_at":"2024-11-08T23:49:53Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"No, you did not mention SortingNetworks.jl above. The function `similar` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types.

   @ Base abstractarray.jl:821
  similar(::AbstractArray, ::Type{T}, ::NTuple{N, Int64}) where {T, N}
   @ Base abstractarray.jl:833
  ...

 [1] repeat_outer(a::UnitRange{Int64}, ::Tuple{Float64})
   @ Base._RepeatInnerOuter ./abstractarraymath.jl:480

   @ Base ./abstractarraymath.jl:357

help?> repeat
search: repeat reset pad_repeat keepat! relpath replace read @deprecate realpath repr RecipeData

  repeat(A::AbstractArray, counts::Integer...)

  Construct an array by repeating array A a given number of times in each dimension, specified by
  counts.

  See also: fill, Iterators.repeated, Iterators.cycle.
```

ERROR: MethodError: no method matching repeat(::UnitRange{Int64}, ::Float64)

Closest candidates are:

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

+"""
+    nth(itr, n::Integer)
+
+Get the `n`th element of an iterable collection. Return `nothing` if not existing.
+
+See also: [`first`](@ref), [`last`](@ref)
+
+# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> nth(2:2:10, 4)
+8
+
+julia> nth(reshape(1:30, (5,6)), 6)
+6
+```
+"""
+nth(itr, n::Integer) = _nth(IteratorSize(itr), itr, n)
+nth(itr::AbstractArray, n::Integer) = n > length(itr) ? nothing : itr[n]","path":"base/iterators.jl","commit_id":"3870e3ea7aeb2f6b7c7a23608d4dcda0af057a8b","original_commit_id":"11ee27a31310d88d4606d8325eafd36fb2559712","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This line `nth(itr::AbstractRange, n) = getindex(itr, n)` will for sure fail on the axes of an OffsetArray. I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56580/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56580/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2480888746","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56580#issuecomment-2480888746","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56580","id":2480888746,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6T31uq","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-17T02:02:13Z","updated_at":"2024-11-17T02:02:13Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This seems like a lot of code!

I reproduced the above benchmark here:
https://gist.github.com/mcabbott/fe2e0821e9bfe5cc7643bb15adf445d0
I get 75.625 μs for `first(Iterators.drop(itr, n-1))` vs 1.558 μs for this version. However, the sum isn't very informative, as it's entirely driven by one case, `Cycle{Vector{Int64}}`. Some other cases are faster, some slower (String). Maybe they ought to be discussed in follow-up PRs? 

No strong position on whether this needs a name or not, but perhaps this first PR can focus on that, and let the implementation be just:
```julia
nth(itr, n::Integer) = first(Iterators.drop(itr, n-1))
nth(itr::AbstractArray, n::Integer) = itr[begin-1+n]
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2480888746/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

@btime begin

julia> x = 1:0.25:4;
julia> xu = 1u"inch":0.25u"inch":4u"inch";
julia> xval = (1u"inch".value):(0.25u"inch".value):(4u"inch".value);

julia> @less Vector{eltype(xu)}(xu)
```
This iterates the range and indexes `@inbounds`:
```julia
function Array{T,1}(r::AbstractRange{T}) where {T}
    a = Vector{T}(undef, length(r))
    i = 1
    for x in r
        @inbounds a[i] = x
        i += 1
    end
    return a
end
```
And the iteration for StepRange assumes that  `prev + step(r)` will exactly hit `last(r)`:
```julia
iterate(r::OrdinalRange) = isempty(r) ? nothing : (first(r), first(r))

function iterate(r::OrdinalRange{T}, i) where {T}
    @inline
    i == last(r) && return nothing
    next = convert(T, i + step(r))
    (next, next)
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2486506248/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I do think an object lying about its `length` is illegal. But that's not the problem here, it's that iteration of a `StepRange <: OrdinalRange` relies on the (documented) assumption that adding `step` repeatedly will exactly hit the endpoint. Which is violated if you ever make a StepRange of things with floating point error.

Certainly the fallback path of `a:b:c` should not produce a StepRange when it's not sure whether the resulting step is kosher. It should probably fall back to StepRangeLen.

Possibly the iteration should be made more robust by checking `>=` instead of `==`, but I don't think that will eliminate all such mistakes.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2489403005/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

example [from Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/gpu-julia-vs-gpu-matlab/122663/61) shows a slowdown when broadcasting a moderately complicated expression, instead of broadcasting a function containing the same expression:
```julia
arrayfun!(C, A, B) = @. C = A^2 + B^2 + A * B + A / B - A * B - A / B + A * B + A / B - A * B - A / B
scalarfun(A::Real, B::Real) = A^2 + B^2 + A * B + A / B - A * B - A / B + A * B + A / B - A * B - A / B

let N = 151
    A, B, C1, C2 = (rand(N,N,N).+1 for _ in 1:4)
    @btime arrayfun!($C1, $A, $B)
    @btime $C2 .= scalarfun.($A, $B)
    C1 ≈ C2
end
#  17.306 ms (11 allocations: 352 bytes)
#   5.900 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```
The effect seems fairly robust, it's not particular to 3D arrays, nor to `A^2`. 
Replacing `@.` with `.+` etc. helps a bit (which according to #29120 removes n-ary `+`, here n<=4):
```julia
arrayfun!(C, A, B) = C .= A.^2 .+ B.^2 .+ A .* B .+ A ./ B .- A .* B .- A ./ B .+ A .* B .+ A ./ B .- A .* B .- A ./ B
#  17.345 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```
Simpler expressions also have the slowdown but no allocation:
```julia
arrayfun!(C, A, B) = @. C = A^2 + B^2 + A * B + A / B
scalarfun(A::Real, B::Real) = A^2 + B^2 + A * B + A / B
#  3.148 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
#  971.000 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```
Even simpler expressions like `arrayfun!(C, A, B) = @. C = A^2 + B^2` show no slowdown at all.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56629/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56629/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

WARNING: Wrapping `Vararg` directly in UnionAll is deprecated (wrap the tuple instead).

go faster"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2020-04-04T19:17:28Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:35:53Z","closed_at":"2022-10-31T05:00:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"While `copy(x::Transpose)` calls a special `transpose!(similar(x, ...), x)`, I think that `copy(x::PermutedDimsArray)` doesn't call a special method. If I try to re-use what `permutedims!` does, then strangely I get something much faster than the original:
```julia
julia> xx = rand(128,128,128);
julia> yy = permutedims(xx, (2,3,1));

julia> @btime copy(PermutedDimsArray($xx, (2,3,1))); 
  17.470 ms (10 allocations: 16.00 MiB) # not sure what version this was, original post.
  22.108 ms (10 allocations: 16.00 MiB) # on Julia 1.3.1
  16.035 ms (10 allocations: 16.00 MiB) # on Julia 1.4.2
  22.730 ms (6 allocations: 16.00 MiB) # on Julia 1.5.2
  26.694 ms (6 allocations: 16.00 MiB) # on Julia 1.6 -- worse!

julia> @btime permutedims($xx, (2,3,1));
  16.195 ms (6 allocations: 16.00 MiB)
  16.040 ms (2 allocations: 16.00 MiB) # on Julia 1.6

julia> function Base.copyto!(dest::AbstractArray{T,N}, src::PermutedDimsArray{T,N,perm,iperm}) where {T,N,perm,iperm}
           checkbounds(dest, axes(src)...)
           P = PermutedDimsArray(dest, iperm)
           Base.PermutedDimsArrays._copy!(P, parent(src))
           dest
       end

julia> @btime copy(PermutedDimsArray($xx, (2,3,1))); # with this new copyto!(similar(xx), xx)
  4.462 ms (22 allocations: 16.00 MiB)
  4.413 ms (13 allocations: 16.00 MiB) # on Julia 1.6

julia> yy == copy(PermutedDimsArray(xx, (2,3,1)))
true

julia> using OMEinsum # for a fast method from TensorOperations, to compare

julia> @btime OMEinsum.tensorpermute($xx, (3,2,1)); # with a different perm, by mistake!
  2.074 ms (48 allocations: 16.00 MiB)

julia> @btime OMEinsum.tensorpermute($xx, (2,3,1)); # added later
  1.972 ms (64 allocations: 16.00 MiB)  # on Julia 1.6
```
Presumably we should add this. But why is `permutedims!` so much slower, when it does the same thing?

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":23,"created_at":"2024-11-16T12:17:36Z","updated_at":"2024-11-27T18:48:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56580","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56580","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56580.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56580.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hi,

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`. (edit: instead of throwing an error, i.e. `first(itr, n)` and `last(itr, n)`)

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

stdlib"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-11-27T18:59:43Z","updated_at":"2024-11-30T04:41:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If the unbound static parameter is not used in the function body, then it should not get reported by `detect_unbound_args`. There are perfectly valid functions that don't use their static parameters. For example:
 
```julia-repl
julia> module Mod
           f(x::NTuple{<:Any, T}, y::NTuple{<:Any, T}) where T = (x..., y...)
       end
Main.Mod

julia> Test.detect_unbound_args(Mod)
[1] f(x::NTuple{var"#s5", T} where var"#s5", y::NTuple{var"#s4", T} where var"#s4") where T @ Main.Mod REPL[14]:2

julia> Mod.f((1,2),(3,4))
(1, 2, 3, 4)

julia> Mod.f((1,2),(3,4.0))
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching f(::Tuple{Int64, Int64}, ::Tuple{Int64, Float64})
The function `f` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types.

Closest candidates are:
  f(::NTuple{var"#s5", T} where var"#s5", ::NTuple{var"#s4", T} where var"#s4") where T
   @ Main.Mod REPL[14]:2

Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[17]:1

julia> Mod.f((),())
()
```

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-09-28T16:49:14Z","updated_at":"2024-10-15T22:25:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55920","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"We have `!f === (!)∘f` to negate any boolean function. Was there ever discussion of making `-f === (-)∘f` too? Both are small conveniences to avoid defining `x -> op(x)`. This one would allow for example:

```julia
julia> A = reshape(1:16,4,4);

julia> eigvals(A; sortby=-real)  # does not accept rev=true
4-element Vector{ComplexF64}:
     36.209372712298546 + 0.0im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 + 1.9341169324764503e-16im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 - 1.9341169324764503e-16im
     -2.209372712298546 + 0.0im

julia> sum(-log, A; dims=1)  # -sum allocates twice
1×4 Matrix{Float64}:
 -3.17805  -7.42655  -9.38261  -10.6846
```

This will not change the fact that `-f.(xs)` means `-(f.(xs))`, when usually fused `@. -f(xs)` would be better. (Unless you write `(-f).(xs)` by hand.)

Needs tests obviously, but RFC?

It's true that `![true, false]` is an error, while `-[1,2,3]` is the same as broadcasting. So it would be somewhat strange if `(-a)(i) != -(a(i))`... assuming that `(-a)` were defined like this PR, and that the array is callable.

The only callable array I can think of is from my package, which uses (ab)uses round brackets to mean another kind of indexing. In this case, the two meanings would agree, since indexing the negated array `-A` is the same as indexing and then negating:
```julia
julia> A = AxisKeys.KeyedArray([10, 20, 30], char='a':'c');

julia> A('b') == A[2] == 20  # 2nd element can be accessed by its label 'b'
true

julia> -A('b') == ((-)∘A)('b') == -20
true
```

The closest related PR is probably #39042, which makes `f^2 === f∘f`. That has a different overlap of ideas with callable arrays, in that `A^2 == A*A` for matrices.  Note that if both PRs were merged, the parser thinks the minus is outside:  `-3^2 == -9`.


I can't picture why defining `+f` would be helpful. Certainly there is general intention to call arbitrary functions on other functions, `g(f) === g∘f`. It is conceivable that some people would want `f-g` to mean `x -> f(x)-g(x)`, and similarly `f+g`, but those seem a big step further, and this PR does not propose them.


Maybe it would be worth thinking up the worst "wat" which this change would allow?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2415276023/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+"""
+    -f::Function
+

+

+

+

+1×2 Matrix{Float64}:
+ 2.0  4.0
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    This method requires at least Julia 1.12.

function to obtain a view of a diagonal of a matrix is useful, and this is clearly being used widely within `LinearAlgebra`.

The implementation here iterates according to the `IndexStyle` of the array:
```julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> A = reshape(1:9, 3, 3)
3×3 reshape(::UnitRange{Int64}, 3, 3) with eltype Int64:
 1  4  7
 2  5  8
 3  6  9

julia> diagview(A,1)
2-element view(::UnitRange{Int64}, 4:4:8) with eltype Int64:
 4
 8

julia> T = Tridiagonal(1:3, 3:6, 4:6)
4×4 Tridiagonal{Int64, UnitRange{Int64}}:
 3  4  ⋅  ⋅
 1  4  5  ⋅
 ⋅  2  5  6
 ⋅  ⋅  3  6

julia> diagview(T,1)
3-element view(::Tridiagonal{Int64, UnitRange{Int64}}, StepRangeLen(CartesianIndex(1, 2), CartesianIndex(1, 1), 3)) with eltype Int64:
 4
 5
 6
```

With `A::Matrix` it will always be fine, but with UpperTriangular you would need to use `diagview(U,0)` to get two views of the same type:
```julia
julia> diagview(A::AbstractMatrix, k::Integer=0) = @view A[diagind(A, k, IndexStyle(A))]  # as in PR

julia> diagview(A::UpperTriangular) = diagview(parent(A))  # proposed optimisation

julia> U = UpperTriangular(A);

julia> Bidiagonal(diagview(U), diagview(U,1), :U)  # not the most informative error!
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching Bidiagonal(::SubArray{…}, ::SubArray{…}, ::Symbol)

julia> Bidiagonal(diagview(U,0), diagview(U,1), :U)  # like this is fine
4×4 Bidiagonal{Float64, SubArray{Float64, 1, UpperTriangular{Float64, Matrix{Float64}}, Tuple{StepRangeLen{CartesianIndex{2}, CartesianIndex{2}, CartesianIndex{2}, Int64}}, false}}:
 0.875899  0.396184   ⋅          ⋅ 
  ⋅        0.466412  0.175884    ⋅ 
  ⋅         ⋅        0.0872872  0.324497

s]"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-10-22T18:40:59Z","updated_at":"2024-10-25T18:20:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As also described in https://github.com/JuliaArrays/StaticArrays.jl/issues/1282, there is a large performance regression with `v1.11` when using `StaticArrays`:

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, StaticArrays, BenchmarkTools

function mwe1(a, X, n)
    K = zeros(SMatrix{3,3})
    for i in 1:n
        k = a * i
        K += k * X * X'
    end
    return K
end

@btime mwe1(1e-5, SVector{3}(1.0, 1.0, 1.0), 1_000_000);
```
```bash
❯ julia +1.10.5 --project -t 6 mwe.jl 
  1.070 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

❯ julia +1.11.1 --project -t 6 mwe.jl 
  129.890 ms (7000000 allocations: 289.92 MiB)
```
Interestingly, the problem can be solved by changing `k * X * X'` to `k * (X * X')`:
```julia
function mwe3(a, X, n)
    K = zeros(SMatrix{3,3})
    for i in 1:n
        k = a * i
        K += k * (X * X')
    end
    return K
end
@btime mwe3(1e-5, SVector{3}(1.0, 1.0, 1.0), 1_000_000);
```
```julia-repl
❯ julia +1.10.5 --project -t 6 mwe.jl 
  805.458 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

❯ julia +1.11.1 --project -t 6 mwe.jl
  708.208 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56296/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56296/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2438539234","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56296#issuecomment-2438539234","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56296","id":2438539234,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6RWSfi","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-25T18:20:18Z","updated_at":"2024-10-25T18:20:18Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"3-arg `*` was new in Julia 1.7, and did have a small negative effect on square SMatrices, a few ns.  convert(::Type{T}, r::AbstractRange) where {T<:AbstractRange} = r isa T ? r : T(r)::T
 
+AbstractRange{T}(r::AbstractRange) where T = T(first(r)):T(step(r)):T(last(r))

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1824651046","pull_request_review_id":2408258902,"id":1824651046,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5swfcm","diff_hunk":"@@ -275,6 +275,10 @@ RangeStepStyle(::Type{<:AbstractRange{<:Integer}}) = RangeStepRegular()
 
 convert(::Type{T}, r::AbstractRange) where {T<:AbstractRange} = r isa T ? r : T(r)::T
 
+AbstractRange{T}(r::AbstractRange) where T = T(first(r)):T(step(r)):T(last(r))
+AbstractArray{T,1}(r::AbstractRange) where T = AbstractRange{T}(r)

Preserving integer steps exactly is a nice-to-have, which you can get by specialising on OrdinalRange. But preserving length of a vector, always, is not optional.

I know nothing about infinite arrays, and am not sure they are fully legal AbstractArrays. But the type you mention seems to be `InfiniteArrays.InfStepRange{T, S} <: OrdinalRange{T, S} <: AbstractRange{T} <: AbstractArray{T, 1} <: Any`, so it would hit a specialisation on OrdinalRange.

```julia
julia> Base.@kwdef struct S  # without this PR, returns S
           a = 1
           b::Int = 2
           c
       end
S

julia> @kwdef struct S  # with this PR, returns kwdef_defaults
           a = 1
           b::Int = 2
           c
       end
kwdef_defaults (generic function with 3 methods)
```
I don't know whether we care, since without the macro a struct definition returns `nothing`
```julia
julia> struct S2  # without macro
           a
           b::Int
           c
       end

julia> ans === nothing
true

julia> struct S3  # without macto
           a
           b::Int
           c
           S3(a::Int,b::Int,c::Int) = new(a,b,c)
       end

values","user":{"login":"aplavin","id":687995,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4Nzk5NQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/687995?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin","html_url":"https://github.com/aplavin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"As of now, there's no way to access default field values passed to @kwdef, aside from parsing the constructor signature (https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-to-get-default-values-of-a-functions-kwargs/66158/2).

This PR adds `Base.kwdef_defaults(::Type) = (default fieldvals)` for all structs defined with `@kwdef`. This function can either be made public immediately, or remain internal for some time.

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50876. Useful in various cases, for example in pretty-printing: knowing the defaults makes it possible for user show() methods to omit fields with default values.

That's what `@kwdef` now expands to:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand @kwdef struct S
               a = 1
               b::Int = 2
               c
       end
quote
    #= REPL[22]:51 =#
    begin
        $(Expr(:meta, :doc))
        struct S
            #= REPL[25]:2 =#
            a
            #= REPL[25]:3 =#
            b::Int
            #= REPL[25]:4 =#
            c
        end
    end
    #= REPL[22]:52 =#
    function S(; a = 1, b = 2, c)
        #= REPL[25]:1 =#
        S(a, b, c)
    end
    #= REPL[22]:53 =#
    Base.kwdef_defaults(::Base.Type{S}) = begin
            #= REPL[22]:53 =#
            (; a = 1, b = 2)
        end
end
```","created_at":"2024-08-27T11:19:36Z","updated_at":"2024-09-06T03:19:33Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"b1186592faac21504039cd4a09f085ee758fc128","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":294535960,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyOTQ1MzU5NjA=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/needs%20tests","name":"needs values","user":{"login":"aplavin","id":687995,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4Nzk5NQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/687995?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin","html_url":"https://github.com/aplavin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"As of now, there's no way to access default field values passed to @kwdef, aside from parsing the constructor signature (https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-to-get-default-values-of-a-functions-kwargs/66158/2).

This PR adds `Base.kwdef_defaults(::Type) = (default fieldvals)` for all structs defined with `@kwdef`. This function can either be made public immediately, or remain internal for some time.

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50876. Useful in various cases, for example in pretty-printing: knowing the defaults makes it possible for user show() methods to omit fields with default values.

That's what `@kwdef` now expands to:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand @kwdef struct S
               a = 1
               b::Int = 2
               c
       end
quote
    #= REPL[22]:51 =#
    begin
        $(Expr(:meta, :doc))
        struct S
            #= REPL[25]:2 =#
            a
            #= REPL[25]:3 =#
            b::Int
            #= REPL[25]:4 =#
            c
        end
    end
    #= REPL[22]:52 =#
    function S(; a = 1, b = 2, c)
        #= REPL[25]:1 =#
        S(a, b, c)
    end
    #= REPL[22]:53 =#
    Base.kwdef_defaults(::Base.Type{S}) = begin
            #= REPL[22]:53 =#
            (; a = 1, b = 2)
        end
end

{"action":"opened","number":55920,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55920","id":2097151674,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM58__66","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55920","number":55920,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Allow function negation `-f` to work like `!f`","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"We have `!f === (!)∘f` to negate any boolean function. Was there ever discussion of making `-f === (-)∘f` too? Both are small conveniences to avoid defining `x -> op(x)`. This one would allow for example:

```julia
julia> A = reshape(1:16,4,4);

julia> eigvals(A; sortby=-real)  # does not accept rev=true
4-element Vector{ComplexF64}:
     36.209372712298546 + 0.0im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 + 1.9341169324764503e-16im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 - 1.9341169324764503e-16im
     -2.209372712298546 + 0.0im

julia> sum(-log, A; dims=1)  # -sum allocates twice
1×4 Matrix{Float64}:
 -3.17805  -7.42655  -9.38261  -10.6846
```

This will not change the fact that `-f.(xs)` means `-(f.(xs))`, when usually fused `@. -f(xs)` would be better. (Unless you write `(-f).(xs)` by hand.)

Needs tests obviously, but RFC?

changes how a `Bidiagonal` is displayed using the 2-arg `show` to a form that may be parsed.
After this,
```julia
julia> B = Bidiagonal([1,2,3], [1,2], :U)
3×3 Bidiagonal{Int64, Vector{Int64}}:
 1  1  ⋅
 ⋅  2  2
 ⋅  ⋅  3

julia> show(B)
Bidiagonal([1, 2, 3], [1, 2], :U)
```
The displayed form is a valid constructor now.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55347/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55347/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2265800942","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55347#issuecomment-2265800942","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55347","id":2265800942,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6HDWDu","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-02T16:59:33Z","updated_at":"2024-08-02T16:59:33Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Note that at present the compact printing of Bidiagonal is quite unlike the other structured matrices:
```julia
julia> (B,)
(3×3 Bidiagonal{Int64, Vector{Int64}}:
 diag: 1  2  3
 super: 1  2,)

julia> (Diagonal([1,2,3]), UpperTriangular(rand(1:9,3,3)))
([1 0 0; 0 2 0; 0 0 3], [3 4 4; 0 9 5; 0 0 8])
```
An alternative nice printing would be `Bidiagonal([1 1 0; 0 2 2; 0 0 3], :U)`, which is also a valid constructor.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2265800942/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

changes the compact printing to preserve more information -- an adjoint vector is not quite a matrix, and Diagonal wastes a lot of space:
```julia
julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # before
([1 0 0 0; 0 2 0 0; 0 0 3 0; 0 0 0 4], [5 6 7], [8 9 10])

julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # after
(Diagonal(1:4), adjoint([5, 6, 7]), transpose(8:10))
```
Would have been better to do at the same time as 1.6's other printing changes, I guess. changes the compact printing to preserve more information -- an adjoint vector is not quite a matrix, and Diagonal wastes a lot of space:
```julia
julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # before
([1 0 0 0; 0 2 0 0; 0 0 3 0; 0 0 0 4], [5 6 7], [8 9 10])

julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # after
(Diagonal(1:4), adjoint([5, 6, 7]), transpose(8:10))
```
Would have been better to do at the same time as 1.6's other printing changes, I guess. position","user":{"login":"MilesCranmer","id":7593028,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc1OTMwMjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7593028?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer","html_url":"https://github.com/MilesCranmer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54653.

Fixes #828.

```julia
julia> using Compat: Fix

julia> map(Fix{3}(muladd, 1.0), 1:3, 4:6)
3-element Vector{Float64}:
  5.0
 11.0
 19.0
```

---

**Multiple dispatch**: This is a bit of an edge case, and presumably many functions in Compat are like this, but since `Fix1` is broadly used:

Note that even with this PR, `Compat.Fix{1}` is not the same as `Base.Fix1` on Julia versions earlier than `1.12.0-DEV.966`. Therefore, if you wish to use this as a way to dispatch on `Fix{N}` for libraries compatible with Julia earlier than v1.12, you should declare a function both for `Compat.Fix{1}` as well as `Base.Fix1`. You can do this with a

```julia
@static if !isdefined(Base, :Fix)
    #= Declare additional methods for Compat.Fix =#
end
```

{"action":"opened","number":55381,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55381","id":2004022666,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM53cvWK","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55381","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55381.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55381.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55381","number":55381,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Simplify `dropdims(::Transpose)` and `insertdims(::PermutedDimsArray)` etc.","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Before, these wrap the wrapper in a ReshapedArray:
```julia
julia> dropdims([1,2,3]'; dims=1)
3-element reshape(adjoint(::Vector{Int64}), 3) with eltype Int64:
 1
 2
 3

julia> insertdims(PermutedDimsArray([1 2; 3 4], (2,1)); dims=2)
2×1×2 reshape(PermutedDimsArray(::Matrix{Int64}, (2, 1)), 2, 1, 2) with eltype Int64:
[:, :, 1] =
 1
 2

[:, :, 2] =
 3
 4
```

After:
```julia
julia> dropdims([1,2,3]'; dims=1)
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 1
 2
 3

julia> insertdims(PermutedDimsArray([1 2; 3 4], (2,1)); dims=2)
2×1×2 PermutedDimsArray(::Array{Int64, 3}, (2, 3, 1)) with eltype Int64:
[:, :, 1] =
 1
 2

[:, :, 2] =
 3
 4
```
This is not always faster, alone, but the hope is that the simpler return type will usually be faster downstream. (Especially with e.g. `CuArray`s, where wrapping twice causes them to miss specialised methods.)
```julia
julia> P13 = PermutedDimsArray(randn(2,1,3,1,4), (4,5,2,1,3));

julia> @btime dropdims($P13; dims=(1,3));
  189.628 ns (10 allocations: 192 bytes)  # before, ReshapedArray(PermutedDimsArray(Array{Float64, 5}))
  272.895 ns (10 allocations: 256 bytes)  # after, PermutedDimsArray(Array{Float64, 3})

julia> A13 = collect(P13);

julia> @btime dropdims($A13; dims=(1,3));
  172.333 ns (11 allocations: 240 bytes)
```
It appears that the compiler can predict the type when `dims` is constant. More stringent tests (or ways to do better) would be welcome:
```julia
julia> drop1(x) = dropdims(x; dims=(1,));

julia> @code_warntype drop1(P13)  # before
MethodInstance for drop1(::PermutedDimsArray{Float64, 5, (4, 5, 2, 1, 3), (4, 3, 5, 1, 2), Array{Float64, 5}})
  from drop1(x) @ Main REPL[12]:1
Arguments
  #self#::Core.Const(drop1)
  x::PermutedDimsArray{Float64, 5, (4, 5, 2, 1, 3), (4, 3, 5, 1, 2), Array{Float64, 5}}
Body::Base.ReshapedArray{Float64, 4, PermutedDimsArray{Float64, 5, (4, 5, 2, 1, 3), (4, 3, 5, 1, 2), Array{Float64, 5}}, NTuple{4, Base.MultiplicativeInverses.SignedMultiplicativeInverse{Int64}}}
1 ─ %1 = (:dims,)::Core.Const((:dims,))
...

julia> @code_warntype drop1(P13)  # after
MethodInstance for drop1(::PermutedDimsArray{Float64, 5, (4, 5, 2, 1, 3), (4, 3, 5, 1, 2), Array{Float64, 5}})
  from drop1(x) @ Main REPL[240]:1
Arguments
  #self#::Core.Const(drop1)
  x::PermutedDimsArray{Float64, 5, (4, 5, 2, 1, 3), (4, 3, 5, 1, 2), Array{Float64, 5}}
Body::PermutedDimsArray{Float64, 4, (4, 2, 1, 3), (3, 2, 4, 1), Array{Float64, 4}}

`Diagonal`","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This changes the compact printing to preserve more information -- an adjoint vector is not quite a matrix, and Diagonal wastes a lot of space:
```julia
julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # before
([1 0 0 0; 0 2 0 0; 0 0 3 0; 0 0 0 4], [5 6 7], [8 9 10])

julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # after
(Diagonal(1:4), adjoint([5, 6, 7]), transpose(8:10))
```
Would have been better to do at the same time as 1.6's other printing changes, I guess. `Diagonal`","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This changes the compact printing to preserve more information -- an adjoint vector is not quite a matrix, and Diagonal wastes a lot of space:
```julia
julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # before
([1 0 0 0; 0 2 0 0; 0 0 3 0; 0 0 0 4], [5 6 7], [8 9 10])

julia> (Diagonal(1:4), [5,6,7]', transpose(8:10))  # after
(Diagonal(1:4), adjoint([5, 6, 7]), transpose(8:10))
```
Would have been better to do at the same time as 1.6's other printing changes, I guess.
