s]"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":16,"created_at":"2018-09-12T13:23:33Z","updated_at":"2022-11-02T01:37:09Z","closed_at":"2022-07-19T13:52:57Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"After some [discussion on discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/alternative-to-mapslices-that-does-not-allocate-slices/13820/7) it was proposed to "feature request" a version of `mapslices` in Julia Base that does not allocate slices. Naively I would think the behavior and syntax should be identical to `mapslices` except that it iterates views rather than slices of the original array (`mapviews` is a tentative name). I think it'd be useful for all cases where one wants to apply `mapslices` with a function with no side effects (or that doesn't mutate the original array) and avoid the extra allocations. A typical use case is for example summing with some filtering condition along some dimension:

`mapviews(v -> sum(Iterators.filter(!isnan, v)), M, dims=2)` 

for which I don't think there's an easy efficient solution in Base Julia right now.

It was pointed out that similar ideas have been implemented in the package [JuliennedArrays](https://bramtayl.github.io/JuliennedArrays.jl/latest/index.html) and there was some discussion of moving some of that to Base, see [this comment](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/23645#issuecomment-328265939).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/29146/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":5,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/29146/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1299435605","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/29146#issuecomment-1299435605","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/29146","id":1299435605,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Nc9BV","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-02T01:37:09Z","updated_at":"2022-11-02T01:37:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This should work on nightly; the new `eachslice` which accepts `dims::Tuple` will be in 1.9.

Mapslices is also faster on 1.9, after #40996... although some cases that inferred when that was merged seem not to now? 

```julia
julia> A = rand(Float32, 1000, 1000, 10);

julia> using BenchmarkTools, Statistics

julia> @btime map(mean, eachslice($A, dims=(1,3), drop=false)) |> size
  15.409 ms (9 allocations: 39.47 KiB)
(1000, 1, 10)

julia> @btime mapslices(mean, $A, dims=2) |> size
  13.128 ms (14915 allocations: 276.56 KiB)
(1000, 1, 10)

julia> @btime mean($A; dims=2) |> size
  1.292 ms (14 allocations: 39.77 KiB)
(1000, 1, 10)

julia> VERSION
v"1.9.0-DEV.1694"
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1299435605/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

statement made here about the `in` operator with `Dicts` is too strong and it has edge cases where it fails:
> Some collections follow a slightly different definition. For example, Sets check whether the item isequal to one of the elements. Dicts look for key=>value pairs, and the key is compared using isequal. To test for the presence of a key in a dictionary, use haskey or k in keys(dict). For these collections, the result is always a Bool and never missing.

On `Sets` the above is utterly true, but the statement fails when the `value` in `key=>value in Dict` is `missing`:
```julia
julia> X = Dict(1=>10, 2=>20, 3=>30, missing=>NaN);

julia> (missing=>10) in X
false

julia> (missing=>NaN) ∈ X
false

julia> (1=>missing) ∈ X
missing

julia> (missing=>missing) ∈ X
missing
```

As the above shows, for `Dict`s, a `Bool` is not always returned. So its best to state this out.

Moreover, since only the `key` is compared using `isequal` for `Dicts`. key should be “`key` (not the `value`)” so emphasis is seen as being placed on `key` statement made here about the `in` operator with `Dicts` is too strong and it has edge cases where it fails:
> Some collections follow a slightly different definition. For example, Sets check whether the item isequal to one of the elements. Dicts look for key=>value pairs, and the key is compared using isequal. To test for the presence of a key in a dictionary, use haskey or k in keys(dict). For these collections, the result is always a Bool and never missing.

On `Sets` the above is utterly true, but the statement fails when the `value` in `key=>value in Dict` is `missing`:
```julia
julia> X = Dict(1=>10, 2=>20, 3=>30, missing=>NaN);

julia> (missing=>10) in X
false

julia> (missing=>NaN) ∈ X
false

julia> (1=>missing) ∈ X
missing

julia> (missing=>missing) ∈ X
missing
```

As the above shows, for `Dict`s, a `Bool` is not always returned. So its best to state this out.

Moreover, since only the `key` is compared using `isequal` for `Dicts`. key should be “`key` (not the `value`)” so emphasis is seen as being placed on `key` statement made here about the `in` operator with `Dicts` is too strong and it has edge cases where it fails:
> Some collections follow a slightly different definition. For example, Sets check whether the item isequal to one of the elements. Dicts look for key=>value pairs, and the key is compared using isequal. To test for the presence of a key in a dictionary, use haskey or k in keys(dict). For these collections, the result is always a Bool and never missing.

On `Sets` the above is utterly true, but the statement fails when the `value` in `key=>value in Dict` is `missing`:
```julia
julia> X = Dict(1=>10, 2=>20, 3=>30, missing=>NaN);

julia> (missing=>10) in X
false

julia> (missing=>NaN) ∈ X
false

julia> (1=>missing) ∈ X
missing

julia> (missing=>missing) ∈ X
missing
```

As the above shows, for `Dict`s, a `Bool` is not always returned. So its best to state this out.

Moreover, since only the `key` is compared using `isequal` for `Dicts`. key should be “`key` (not the `value`)” so emphasis is seen as being placed on `key` statement made here about the `in` operator with `Dicts` is too strong and it has edge cases where it fails:
> Some collections follow a slightly different definition. For example, Sets check whether the item isequal to one of the elements. Dicts look for key=>value pairs, and the key is compared using isequal. To test for the presence of a key in a dictionary, use haskey or k in keys(dict). For these collections, the result is always a Bool and never missing.

On `Sets` the above is utterly true, but the statement fails when the `value` in `key=>value in Dict` is `missing`:
```julia
julia> X = Dict(1=>10, 2=>20, 3=>30, missing=>NaN);

julia> (missing=>10) in X
false

julia> (missing=>NaN) ∈ X
false

julia> (1=>missing) ∈ X
missing

julia> (missing=>missing) ∈ X
missing
```

As the above shows, for `Dict`s, a `Bool` is not always returned. So its best to state this out.

Moreover, since only the `key` is compared using `isequal` for `Dicts`. key should be “`key` (not the `value`)” so emphasis is seen as being placed on `key` {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47377","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47377/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47377/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47377/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47377","id":1427885798,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5ByHiH","number":47377,"title":"eachfiber and fibercat methods to work with tensor fibers","user":{"login":"nlw0","id":161042,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTA0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/161042?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0","html_url":"https://github.com/nlw0","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2022-10-28T21:58:29Z","updated_at":"2022-11-02T20:53:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47377","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47377","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47377.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47377.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Fibers are one-dimensional views that generalize columns, rows and "tubes" for higher dimensions in a tensor (multi-dimensional array). eg `A[i,j,:,k,m]`, with a single `:`.

This PR is inspired by the generalization of `eachslice` for generic `dims`, and also the addition of `stack`, which is the inverse of 'eachslice` for dims::Integer.

Here's the rationale for the proposed functions, which i prefix by saying it's mostly based on my personal experience.

When we work with multidimensional arrays, some of the most common operations to use are:
 - `eachslice(A, dims=ndims(A))`
 - `stack(aa)`, which is the inverse of the above (data appended on a new dimension by default)
 - taking fibers, from the first dimension by default.
 - forming a tensor from an array of vectors, which become the columns by default.

It's great that `eachslice` is now generic, but there's a reason `dims::Integer` is a special case that was the only one available. It's both relevant and handy. I think it would be useful to also have the corresponding methods that do the same for a single dimension, but creating a tensor of vectors instead of a vector of tensors. And the first dimension should be the "default" one.

Using the extreme dimensions as default means we can reshape the array, then do the operation over a single dimension, and reshape the data again if desired. It can be a handier way to deal with this than by specifying `dims` with a tuple.

Completely generic `dims` is great to have, but in practice the first and last dimensions tend to be "privileged" somehow. Also, increasing the number of dimensions makes it pretty hard to write down all the dimensions in order to take fibers using `eachslice`. `eachfiber` could eventually be merely `eachslice` itself, except what we specify is the complement of `dims`.

To summarize, the point is to highlight specific use-cases of `eachslice`. In the one hand the old behavior braking down along a single dimension, and now the other extreme of breaking down into 1-dimensional views. Furthermore, the last dimension should be the default for the first case, as the first dimension is the default for the second case. And then apart from that we have the inverse functions that assemble back the tensors from that. The completely generic cases are nice to have, but these two extremes should be highlighted, with the appropriate defaults.

I now suffix that by saying I bet a milkshake my implementation is far from optimally efficient, and that I don't have a great attachment to any specific function names. Could we do everything with some different arguments to `eachslice` or `stack` or `hvncat`? Maybe, I can live with that, I'm more concerned with having the functionality available.

implements `can_setindex` and `can_change_size`, which provide more clear information that is related to `ismutable` but isn't necessarily the same.
Knowing that the length can change may invalidate bounds checking assumptions and collections that can use `setindex!` aren't threadsafe (#41214, #40096).
Helpful for making optionally mutating operations generic (#30407).
Sometimes we have a type where we want to disallow these mutating behaviors even if there is somehow generic support for them.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46500/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46500/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1307290533","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46500#issuecomment-1307290533","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46500","id":1307290533,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5N66ul","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-08T14:15:27Z","updated_at":"2022-11-08T14:15:27Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think that some tests / traits for such properties would be valuable. And the reason to have them in Base is to make extending them frictionless. But they need to be handled more carefully than this PR does.

```
    can_change_size(::Type{T}) -> Bool

Returns `true` if instances of `T` may change size through operations such as `pop!` and
`popfirst!`.
```

The rough idea is clear, it's to distinguish things like Vector from things like SVector & UnitRange. But what *exactly* does "may change size" mean?

`true` could be offering a guarantee that operations like `pop!` will work. Then it must fail to `false` on unknown types. However, this cannot be known from the type alone:
```julia
julia> v = [1,2,3,4]; m = reshape(v, 2,2); pop!(v)  # Vector{Int} is not enough
ERROR: cannot resize array with shared data
```
Alternatively, `false` could be offering a guarantee that `pop!` will *not* work, and hence that an instance cannot be changed by someone else while you weren't looking. Then it must default to `true`. That's what you hint at above:

> Knowing that the length can change may invalidate bounds checking

Returning `Bool` is limiting, maybe it should return a trait, like how `IteratorSize` can return `SizeUnknown`? But acting on a type means Vector is unknown. Can it act on an instance instead, and return `Union{Bool, Nothing}`? What's best may depend on what this is to be used for. What is this to be used for?

(And why say "change size" not length, given that it accepts non-array collections, and `size(Dict(1=>2))` is an error?)


```
    can_setindex(::Type{T}) -> Bool

Query whether a type can use `setindex!`.
```

Again, the rough idea is clear, but what *exactly* does `true` or `false` promise?

> collections that can use `setindex!` aren't threadsafe

Some are safe, some aren't.

What's envisaged for `CuArray`s (which allow mutation, but not one element at a time).

Can what's desired be known from the type or does it needs an instance, and the proposed indices? It might want to be a close cousin of `checkbounds` except for writing not reading.

```julia
julia> x = Bidiagonal([1,2,3], [4,5], :L)
3×3 Bidiagonal{Int64, Vector{Int64}}:
 1  ⋅  ⋅
 4  2  ⋅
 ⋅  5  3

julia> x[2,1] = 10;  # this is OK, but typeof(x) doesn't know about :L

julia> x[1,2] = 10
ERROR: ArgumentError: cannot set entry (1, 2) off the lower bidiagonal band to a nonzero value (10)

julia> checkbounds(Bool, x, 1, 3)  # tells you about getindex
true

julia> d = Dict(1=>:A, 2=>:B);

julia> d[1:2] = [:C, :D]  # only some kinds of `setindex!` work here
ERROR: MethodError: Cannot `convert` an object of type UnitRange{Int64} to an object of type Int64

julia> Base.setindex((:a, :b, :c), :Z, 3)  # can setindex, just not setindex!
(:a, :b, :Z)
```

function","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/maths","name":"maths","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":43,"created_at":"2021-01-02T13:08:04Z","updated_at":"2022-11-08T18:09:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/39071","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I think it would be useful to have an easy way to make a range of points spaced logarithmically. This PR adds the following:
```julia
julia> LogRange(0.03, 810, 7)  # after some updates
7-element LogRange{Float64}:
 0.03, 0.164317, 0.9, 4.9295, 27.0, 147.885, 810.0

julia> LogRange(0.03, 27, 5) |> collect
5-element Vector{Float64}:
  0.03
  0.16431676725154984
  0.9
  4.929503017546495
 27.0
```
Before Julia 1.0, there was a `logspace` function which did something different, and less useful:
```julia
julia> logspace(1, 4, 4, base=2)
4-element Array{Float64,1}:
  2.0
  4.0
  8.0
 16.0
```
This was removed in #25896 in favour of just writing `2 .^ (1:4)`. If you know what *powers* you want, that is fine. But if you know what *start and endpoints* you want, then it's more of a hassle to write something like `2 .^ range(log2(2), log2(16), length=4)`. Well, for powers of 2, I think we can all do that in our heads, but if you want 20 plot points between about 3.5 and 1700, that's when this would be neat. 

After deciding whether or not this is a good idea at all:

* ~~I've given this the same notation as #39069 but it could equally well be `logrange(lo, hi; length)` instead, or in addition.~~
* Matlab's `logspace` behaves like the old one, so perhaps best to avoid that name. As @mkitti points out below, Python also has similar `logspace`, and a `geomspace` which is like this. That's another possible name. Mathematica's `PowerRange[a,b,r]` takes start, stop, and ratio instead. (This one could be upgraded to accept keywords `ratio=1.1` instead of `length=10`, like `range`, but I'm not sure that's often useful.)
* ~~Ideally it would use something like TwicePrecision to hit the endpoints more precisely? If it is to be an iterator, then maybe it should iterate by multiplication, not by addition & `exp`.~~

### Edit:

The current implementation is a `LogRange` struct. Like `LinRange`, this should hit the endpoints exactly, but not always rational intermediate points. I think this is a reasonable trade-off, and matches what was asked succinctly [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071#issuecomment-756407033).

This thread got quite long, sorry. There are about a dozen implementations (here and in a linked gist), many trying harder to hit intermediate points, trying to figure out what was possible. Too many examples also show powers of 10, as that's an eay place to see how often you hit the exact number... these are a distraction, I think.

Perhaps I should also stress that the definition of this is mathematically very simple. Just as `LinRange(lo, hi, 3)` is the two ends plus their arithmetic mean, so `LogRange(lo, hi, 3)` is the two ends plus the geometric mean. For any quantity which is commonly plotted on a log scale, or measured in dB, this is the natural way to divide up the space between `lo` and `hi`. No mention of a base is necessary. No magic inference about whether `0.1` really means `1//10` is needed either.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39071/reactions","total_count":19,"+1":14,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":5,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39071/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1307633814","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071#issuecomment-1307633814","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39071","id":1307633814,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5N8OiW","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-08T18:09:32Z","updated_at":"2022-11-08T18:09:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Thanks all for taking a look, all of a sudden! I still think this would be nice to have.

---

>  implementation just uses the general `mapview(f, A)` "lazy map" mechanism

The strategy there is to explicitly test whether you are an endpoint, and return the exact value. Rather than arranging for the calculation to be guaranteed to produce that value. I believe this is not among the many ideas considered above.

The advantage of the PR's interpolation strategy is that it produces higher accuracy at other points. Here's a test:

```julia
function countepsfrom(x::T, xtrue) where {T<:AbstractFloat}
    target = T(xtrue)
    for n in Iterators.flatten(zip(0:100, -1:-1:-100))
        nextfloat(x, n) === target && return n
    end
    nf = (target - x) / eps(x)
    return isfinite(nf) ? round(Int, nf) : nf
end
function rangestats(fun, lo::Real, hi::Real, rest...; kw...)
    xs = fun(lo, hi, rest...; kw...)
    lo == xs[1] && hi == xs[end] || @warn "does not hit ends"
    all(x -> lo<=x<=hi, xs) || @warn "some values outside range!" maxlog=1
    truth = fun(big(lo), big(hi), rest...; kw...)
    cs = countepsfrom.(xs, truth)
    (avg = sum(abs, cs) / length(cs), worst = maximum(abs, cs))
end

rangestats(range, 0.001, 100, length=1000)  # (avg = 0.0, worst = 0)
rangestats(LinRange, 0.001, 100, 1000)      # (avg = 0.32, worst = 1)
rangestats(LogRange, 0.001, 100, 1000)      # (avg = 0.338, worst = 2)

rangestats(range, -1f20, 1f20, length=10^5)  # (avg = 2.63043, worst = 24577), from near zero!

using FlexiMaps
flexilog = (a, b, l) -> maprange(log, a, b, length=l)

rangestats(LogRange, floatmin(Float32), floatmax(Float32), 1000)  # (avg = 0.0, worst = 0)
rangestats(flexilog, floatmin(Float32), floatmax(Float32), 1000)  # (avg = 21.588, worst = 99)

rangestats(LogRange, 1e-20, 1e20, 1000)  # (avg = 0.347, worst = 2), similar at 1e200
rangestats(flexilog, 1e-20, 1e20, 1000)  # (avg = 6.433, worst = 34), worse at 1e200
```

---

> can probably be made faster and more accurate by using the extended precision `log` and `exp` that I introduced for the pure Julia floating point `pow`

Oh nice, I didn't know these were now available.

The gist above has an implementation using DoubleFloats.jl. It appears to be very accurate, but is it *guaranteed* to be exact at the ends? (For the present state of the PR, I wrote up what you must assume about floating point to prove this, above.) Quick tests:

```julia
geomrange7(1, 2, 10) ≈ LogRange(1, 2, 10)

rangestats(geomrange7, 1e-200, 1e200, 10^5)  # (avg = 0.0, worst = 0)
```

The gist times that version as being 15-60 times slower than the one in the PR. I don't know whether that's a fair estimate for these Base.Math functions too, do you? It's not immediately obvious how to use them to quickly try. Timing the DoubleFloats.jl version today:

```julia
julia> @btime sum(x) setup=(x=LogRange(0.001, 1000.0, 10^6))  # this PR, interpolation
  31.160 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
7.238276888743784e7

julia> @btime sum(x) setup=(x=geomrange7(0.001, 1000.0, 10^6))  # Double64, log + exp
  410.248 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
7.238276888743784e7
```

For linear ranges, `LinRange` vs `range` is the divide between simple and accurate. Although `range` is only about 30% slower, I think, but certainly more opaque. It's possible that a high-precision version should correspondingly be lower-case `logrange(lo, hi; length)` here.

An upgrade may wish to store pre-processed things like StepRangeLen does, not start/stop/length like the present struct. One advantage of the simple version is that `loginterp` can easily be overloaded e.g. because `(1u"m")^(4/6) * (1000u"m")^(2/6)` has awful units.

---

> The only operation used in the implementation is exponentiation so I prefer the term ExpRange

Re the bikeshed, I agree there's an argument that such sequences are the exponentiation of a linear range (although this is currently implemented as a power, which needs both `log` and `exp`). But for better or worse English has chosen "log" not "exp" for such things. Maybe it's a passive/active point-of-view thing, but thankfully only one convention is widespread. We could equally well talk about "exp scale plots", and ["semi-exp graph paper"](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=isch&q=semilog+graph+paper), but do not. We could talk about "points evenly spaced in their exponents" but instead say "evenly spaced in log". Searching for "decibel scale is exponential" finds many people saying things like "is a logarithmic scale" as the standard usage.

The natural pairings are "arithmetic/geometric" (mean, interpolation) and "linear/log" (spacing, plot). Python has a `geomspace` (like this PR) but no `arithspace`... I presume this was because they used up `logspace` for the much less useful thing where you specify the powers (like julia 0.6), and needed one more name.

Within Julia, the precise name `LogRange` in analogy to `LinRange` seems to have been [invented independently](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/59985035/does-there-exist-any-alternative-of-logspace-in-julia-v1-3-1) by someone hoping it would exist. The old `logspace` really involved less `log`, but (I presume) got its name from the same idea that "logarithmic scale" is the natural term.

> over the choices made for LinRange, feel free to change error strings etc. there

Can do, but will make a different PR. Some are outright bugs:

```julia
julia> LinRange(1,2,1)
ERROR: ArgumentError: range(1.0, stop=2.0, length=1): endpoints differ
```

 """
 mod(i::Integer, r::OneTo) = mod1(i, last(r))
 mod(i::Integer, r::AbstractUnitRange{<:Integer}) = mod(i-first(r), length(r)) + first(r)

+"""
+    LogRange(start, stop, length)
+
+Construct a specialized array whose elements are spaced logarithmically
+between the given endpoints. That is, the ratio of successive elements is
+a constant, calculated from the length.
+
+Like [`LinRange`](@ref), floating-point error may cause it to miss intermediate
+rational values, but the first and last elements should be exactly those provided.
+
+This is similar to `geomspace` in Python. Unlike `PowerRange` in Mathematica,
+you specify the number of elements not the ratio.
+Unlike `logspace` in Python and Matlab, the `start` and `stop` arguments are
+always the first and last elements of the result, not powers applied to some base.
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.9"
+    This function requires at least Julia 1.9.
+
+# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> LogRange(10, 4000, 3)
+3-element LogRange{Float64}:
+ 10.0, 200.0, 4000.0
+
+julia> ans[2] ≈ sqrt(10 * 4000)  # middle element is the geometric mean
+true
+
+julia> LogRange(-62_500, -0.01, 5) |> collect  # endpoints are always exact
+5-element Vector{Float64}:
+ -62500.0
+  -1250.0
+    -25.0
+     -0.49999999999999994
+     -0.01
+
+julia> LogRange(√2, 32, 10)
+10-element LogRange{Float64}:
+ 1.41421, 2.0, 2.82843, 4.0, 5.65685, 8.0, 11.3137, 16.0, 22.6274, 32.0
+
+julia> LogRange(1, 1000, 4) ≈ 10 .^ (0:3)  # was logspace(0,3,4) in Julia < 1.0
+true
+
+julia> LogRange(1, -1+0im, 5) ≈ cis.(LinRange(0, pi, 5))  # complex numbers
+true
+```

+struct LogRange{T} <: AbstractArray{T,1}
+    start::T
+    stop::T

The main goal was StepRangeLen, because other changes caused these to be produced in cases where StepRange was previously used, and some people wanted a StepRange of BigInt:
```julia
julia> StepRange(1,2,3) * 4 |> typeof
StepRange{Int64,Int64}                    # julia 1.0
StepRangeLen{Int64, Int64, Int64, Int64}  # now, to allow for zero step
```
I'm not entirely sure why LinRange was changed there, since floating point ranges longer than `Int` seem hard to imagine. But perhaps someone may convert a StepRange to a LinRange and want this to always work -- every AbstractRange can be converted to LinRange, perhaps you want to standardise there for some purpose.

Is there any conceivable use for a LogRange longer than Int? There is no path to inadvertantly convert a StepRange etc.
```julia
julia> StepRange(big(1),2,big(3)) |> length |> typeof
BigInt

julia> exp.(StepRange(big(1),2,big(3))) |> length |> typeof
Int64
```
If not, I'm inclined to say we should prefer the simplicity of `Int` over the symmetry to AbstractRange types. (And probably standardise indices on `Int` too, as suggested below.)

One concern besides simplicity is whether the guarantee that the endpoints will always be exact still holds. I don't have a failure case, but BigInt length will produce things like this as intermediate steps:
```julia
julia> 0.01 ^ (big(1) / 1)
0.01000000000000000020816681711721685132943093776702880859375
```","created_at":"2022-11-08T18:10:17Z","updated_at":"2022-11-08T18:10:18Z","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071#discussion_r1016965982","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/39071","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","_links":{"self":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1016965982"},"html":{"href":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071#discussion_r1016965982"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/39071"}},"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1016965982/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"start_line":null,"original_start_line":null,"start_side":null,"line":1534,"original_line":1534,"side":"RIGHT","in_reply_to_id":1016492199},"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/39071","id":547752343,"node_id":"MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NTQ3NzUyMzQz","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39071","number":39071,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add function","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I think it would be useful to have an easy way to make a range of points spaced logarithmically. This PR adds the following:
```julia
julia> LogRange(0.03, 810, 7)  # after some updates
7-element LogRange{Float64}:
 0.03, 0.164317, 0.9, 4.9295, 27.0, 147.885, 810.0

julia> LogRange(0.03, 27, 5) |> collect
5-element Vector{Float64}:
  0.03
  0.16431676725154984
  0.9
  4.929503017546495
 27.0
```
Before Julia 1.0, there was a `logspace` function which did something different, and less useful:
```julia
julia> logspace(1, 4, 4, base=2)
4-element Array{Float64,1}:
  2.0
  4.0
  8.0
 16.0
```
This was removed in #25896 in favour of just writing `2 .^ (1:4)`. If you know what *powers* you want, that is fine. But if you know what *start and endpoints* you want, then it's more of a hassle to write something like `2 .^ range(log2(2), log2(16), length=4)`. Well, for powers of 2, I think we can all do that in our heads, but if you want 20 plot points between about 3.5 and 1700, that's when this would be neat. 

After deciding whether or not this is a good idea at all:

* ~~I've given this the same notation as #39069 but it could equally well be `logrange(lo, hi; length)` instead, or in addition.~~
* Matlab's `logspace` behaves like the old one, so perhaps best to avoid that name. As @mkitti points out below, Python also has similar `logspace`, and a `geomspace` which is like this. That's another possible name. Mathematica's `PowerRange[a,b,r]` takes start, stop, and ratio instead. (This one could be upgraded to accept keywords `ratio=1.1` instead of `length=10`, like `range`, but I'm not sure that's often useful.)
* ~~Ideally it would use something like TwicePrecision to hit the endpoints more precisely? If it is to be an iterator, then maybe it should iterate by multiplication, not by addition & `exp`.~~

### Edit:

The current implementation is a `LogRange` struct. Like `LinRange`, this should hit the endpoints exactly, but not always rational intermediate points. I think this is a reasonable trade-off, and matches what was asked succinctly [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071#issuecomment-756407033).

This thread got quite long, sorry. There are about a dozen implementations (here and in a linked gist), many trying harder to hit intermediate points, trying to figure out what was possible. Too many examples also show powers of 10, as that's an eay place to see how often you hit the exact number... these are a distraction, I think.

Perhaps I should also stress that the definition of this is mathematically very simple. Just as `LinRange(lo, hi, 3)` is the two ends plus their arithmetic mean, so `LogRange(lo, hi, 3)` is the two ends plus the geometric mean. For any quantity which is commonly plotted on a log scale, or measured in dB, this is the natural way to divide up the space between `lo` and `hi`. No mention of a base is necessary. No magic inference about whether `0.1` really means `1//10` is needed
